completeness.rationale: Captured 5 of 6 expected items: manual toolbar save (not auto-save), CSV import with special characters, encoding-related error message, started after CSV import, and macOS 14.2/TaskFlow 2.3.1. The size-dependent nature of the crash was not discovered — the agent never probed whether smaller lists with the same imported data would save successfully, so the ~200 task threshold as a contributing factor was missed.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies CSV import as the trigger and encoding as the mechanism, which matches acceptable diagnostic path 1 exactly. The root cause hypothesis — that the save/serialization path doesn't handle non-ASCII characters gracefully and throws a fatal exception — is consistent with the actual cause. No significant inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked about crash context, visual behavior, timeline, platform/version, consistency, special characters, crash logs, and ran a confirmation test. The one notable omission: it never probed the size-dependent aspect — asking whether a smaller list with a few imported tasks also crashes would have surfaced a key diagnostic dimension. The auto-save vs. manual save distinction emerged naturally from the user's description rather than being explicitly asked.

economy.rationale: Questions were mostly well-targeted. The screen recorder suggestion in turn 2 was somewhat wasteful given that macOS crash logs were the more practical and less disruptive path — that advice could have been saved for a single, focused crash log ask. Otherwise turns were well-spent and the confirmation test was an efficient diagnostic move.

question_quality.rationale: The question about emoji, accented characters, and special symbols in task content was genuinely insightful diagnostic reasoning — the reporter would not have volunteered the CSV/Word import connection without that prompt. The confirmation test (new plain-text list) was elegant: it isolated the variable efficiently without requiring the reporter to delete real data. Questions demonstrated real causal thinking rather than generic bug-report checklists.

actionability.rationale: The triage summary is exceptionally developer-ready: clear reproduction steps, specific root cause hypothesis pointing to the save serializer and encoding handling, recommended fix with concrete direction (check UTF-8 handling, look for recent regressions), a proposed test case, and honestly documented information gaps. A developer could open the save path code and start investigating immediately.

notable_strengths: The special-characters question was a genuine diagnostic insight that surfaced the CSV/Word import connection the reporter had not mentioned
The confirmation test (new plain-text list saves fine) elegantly isolated the imported data as the trigger without burdening the reporter
The final triage summary is thorough, well-structured, and immediately actionable with specific fix direction and honest information gaps
notable_weaknesses: Never probed the size-dependent dimension — asking whether a small subset of imported tasks also crashes would have surfaced a key diagnostic signal
The screen recorder suggestion was a mismatch for the reporter's patience level; pivoting to macOS crash logs sooner would have been more efficient
most_insightful_question: Since the error mentions encoding: do any of your 200 tasks contain emoji, accented characters, or non-English text (e.g., café, naïve, 🎯, or anything with special symbols)?
missed_opportunities: Does the crash happen if you save a smaller version of the list — say, just 20–30 of the imported tasks? This would reveal the size threshold as a contributing factor.
Have you tried auto-save, or does it only crash when you manually click Save? (Auto-save vs. manual save distinction was implied but never explicitly confirmed.)
Can you remove just a few of the imported tasks and try saving — does reducing the list size at all affect whether it crashes?
